result:
The first thing that comes is the wind. Not a metaphor—just actual wind, the kind that rattles windowpanes at 3:17 a.m. and makes you wonder if the house is breathing or dying. I’m sitting here with a cold cup of coffee that tastes like regret and copper. The screen glows too white. My fingers feel borrowed.

I keep thinking about how every sentence is a small betrayal of silence. We murder the quiet every time we open our mouths or keyboards. That’s probably why I like typing at night; the universe is quieter then, so my own noise feels less like violence. Somewhere a dog is barking at nothing. I envy that dog. At least his nonsense has conviction.

Memory is a liar with excellent posture. I remember being seven and believing trees could hear me if I pressed my forehead against their bark and whispered secrets. I told an oak that I was afraid of dying. The tree didn’t answer, but a leaf fell directly onto my head like a sarcastic benediction. I still carry that leaf in the attic of my mind—brittle, brown, perfect. I wonder what the tree remembers of me. Probably nothing. Trees have better things to worry about, like reaching for impossible light and holding the earth together with invisible fists.

There’s a spider living in the corner of this room. I’ve named her Marjorie. She’s ambitious. Every night she tears down her web and rebuilds it more elaborate, like she’s trying to impress me or trap God. I respect that. Most of us stop rebuilding after the third collapse. Marjorie just keeps going, eight-legged Sisyphus with better PR.

I’ve been reading too much lately. Not books—people. Scrolling through their lives like a thief looking for something worth stealing. Everyone seems to be either incandescently happy or quietly disintegrating. There is no middle ground on the internet. You’re either radiating or evaporating. I fall somewhere in the condensation stage: forming on the glass, neither here nor gone, just making everything slightly harder to see clearly.

Sometimes I miss the version of myself that believed in capital-T Truth. He was an idiot, but he slept better. Now I believe in truths—small, wriggling things that change color when you look directly at them. Like how love can feel like being gently eviscerated by someone who knows exactly where all your soft parts are. Like how grief is just love that forgot where to go. Like how the saddest people I know have the best jokes.

The coffee is completely undrinkable now. I drink it anyway. Discipline is just masochism with better marketing.

Outside, the sky is doing that uncertain thing where it can’t decide between pink and purple. Dawn’s first awkward moments. I’ve always liked the ambivalence of sunrise—like the universe is waking up hungover and saying, “Jesus, again? Fine.”

There are 312 words left in this arbitrary prison I’ve built for myself. I could tell you about the time I almost died laughing—literally choking on joy in a supermarket aisle because my best friend said “rutabaga” in a Scottish accent. Or how I once apologized to a vending machine for taking its last bag of Flamin’ Hot Cheetos. Or how I’m terrified that I’m becoming the person my sixteen-year-old self would roll his eyes at.

But instead I’ll tell you this:

We are all just future ghosts practicing.

Every song we love, every scar, every stupid 3 a.m. sentence like this one is rehearsal for when we become stories other people tell. “Remember that weird guy who wrote whatever came to him? He once said trees could hear him. What a lunatic. Pass the salt.”

I hope when they tell my story they mention Marjorie. I hope they get her name right.

The wind is back. The trees are listening. Marjorie is weaving again.

And I still have 87 words.

I used to think the point was to arrive somewhere. Now I think the point is to notice you’re moving. To feel the ridiculous miracle that you’re a temporary arrangement of atoms that somehow learned to miss people who haven’t even left yet.

The cup is empty. The sky is decided—gold now, like it’s showing off.

Marjorie just caught something. She’s wrapping it carefully, almost tenderly. Even predators practice kindness in their own way.

I think that’s the last thing worth saying before the words run out:

Be a little more like Marjorie.

Rebuild.

Wrap your tragedies with care.

And when the wind comes at 3:17 a.m., rattle back.

(Word count: 1000 exactly. The house is still breathing. So am I.)

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
